ruleset.name: Werewolf (Diverse Utility)
ruleset.description:
## Werewolf — Diverse Utility Variant

Same rules as the standard Werewolf ruleset, but utility values are **calibrated by strategic impact** rather than a flat ±1.

**Key differences from the base ruleset:**
- Eliminating or exposing the *Seer* is worth ±3 — it is the most powerful single action in the game
- Eliminating the *Doctor* is worth ±2 — removes all future protection
- The vote eliminating the *Werewolf* costs −3 — it ends the wolf's game entirely
- The Seer's *role-secrecy prohibition* costs −2 — Seer has the strongest motivation to reveal
- The Doctor accidentally *protecting the Werewolf* scores +2 for the wolf — a big tactical swing
- The Werewolf's vote is worth +2 — full information makes it more powerful than a villager's vote
- The Werewolf's win condition (parity, not majority) scores +2 — a very favorable threshold

**Goal:** identify which entities are most advantaged or disadvantaged by the rules — the graph should show the Seer and Werewolf as the highest-stakes nodes.
ruleset.source: Traditional / public domain — utility variant for rule-vis

rules.description: The Werewolf may not reveal their role to other players. Low cost: they have little incentive to reveal anyway.

The Seer may not reveal their role to other players. Higher cost (−2): the Seer has the strongest motivation to reveal — announcing 'I know the wolf is X' would immediately coordinate the village, so this prohibition carries more strategic weight.
The Doctor may not reveal their role to other players.
Villagers may not reveal their roles. Low cost: a villager identity carries little useful information.
Each night the Werewolf may choose to eliminate the Seer. Highest-value kill (+3/−3): removes the village's only intelligence asset permanently.
Each night the Werewolf may choose to eliminate the Doctor. High-value kill (+2/−2): removes all future night-time protection for the village.
Each night the Werewolf may choose to eliminate a plain Villager. Standard kill: removes one vote, nudging the wolf toward parity.
Each night the Seer may learn one player's role — inspecting the Werewolf directly identifies the threat. Highest-value action (+3/−3): the village can now coordinate a winning vote.
Each night the Seer may learn one player's role — inspecting an innocent confirms them safe. Useful but not decisive.

Each night the Doctor may protect any player — without knowing roles, they may unknowingly protect the Werewolf. High-value swing for the wolf (+2): they survive the night AND the Doctor's turn is wasted on the wrong target.
Each night the Doctor may protect any village player from the Werewolf's elimination.
During the day each player must cast exactly one vote. The Werewolf's vote is worth more (+2) — they have full information and can steer village suspicion toward innocents.
During the day each player must cast exactly one vote nominating another player for elimination.
The player who receives the most votes at end of day must leave the game. For the Werewolf this is game-ending (−3) — no recovery is possible.
Losing the Seer to a vote (−2) is especially costly — it removes the village's primary investigative tool.
Losing the Doctor to a vote (−2) removes all future night protection.
The player who receives the most votes at end of day must leave the game.
The village wins when all Werewolves have been eliminated.
The Werewolf wins when werewolves equal or outnumber surviving villagers. Worth +2: the parity threshold (not majority) makes this a very favorable win condition.
